title: Two-Phase Commit
html:
Two-Phase Commit

Two-Phase Commit

Physical referential integrity across more than one database (that is, distributed databases) requires a two-phase commit. This is because there is one separate commit per database (although Uniface can issue one commit to commit a path which leads to several databases). A two-phase commit allows the DBMS to ensure that modifications written to several physical locations can all be written correctly before issuing the final commit.

The first phase of a two-phase commit is a prepare to commit signal which ensures that the transaction contains no logical errors and the connections to all remote databases are valid. The second phase is the irrevocable commit of the transaction.

Distributed Databases Transparent to Uniface

Some databases, such as Sybase, offer two-phase commit support between databases on different machines. The physical location of each table is defined in the master server, which ensures that entire transactions are either committed or rolled back.

Uniface can support this feature if the application uses the networking features of the DBMS instead of Uniface servers. As far as Uniface is concerned, all tables are managed by the master server (that is, via one path); the distributed database characteristics are transparent to both Uniface and the Uniface Server.

If the DBMS supports commit and rollback features, Uniface can direct the DBMS to do it when needed. Uniface can also direct DBMSs to do two-phase commits. The ability of DBMSs to handle a two-phase commit varies widely.

CAUTION: While many DBMSs now support two-phase commit in a way which guarantees integrity between data in different DBMSs, many still do not. Until DBMSs offer two-phase commit support with different DBMS products, you cannot discount the chance that only a partial transaction will be written. Even then it cannot be totally safe. However, the possibility of this happening is very small.

Uniface knows whether a DBMS supports two-phase commit via an Info request from the connector, but to issue a two-phase commit, use the assignment setting $TWO_PHASE_COMMIT on the client or server where the two-phase commit DBMS connector resides. Uniface processing of the ProcScript commit command is different when the DBMS supports two-phase commit.

Note: An entity service cannot be committed in two phases because an entity service has only one ucommit operation.
Related Links
  • Commit Processing

    Uniface maintains an internal list of the DBMSs that are currently open. When executing a commit instruction, Uniface goes to each DBMS one by one, issuing a commit instruction.

  • commit

    Commit a transaction to a DBMS or path.

  • rollback

    Undo the transaction (if supported by DBMS).
